text: Hello, this is Dr. Jimmy Hsu and this is my video series on the use of the robotic platform on emergent cases.
This is a case of a perforated diverticulitis.
I want to start out just talking a little bit about port placement.
The surgeon places ports in a diagonal line from the left upper quadrant to the right lower quadrant of the abdomen, preparing for robotic-assisted surgery.
And I have a planned space for our ostomy.
However, most of the time in these cases, we can't have stoma markings or anything like that.
One of the things that might be a little strange is that I only place three ports.
I don't usually plan on placing three ports.
However, having a really good first assist at the bedside

is almost as good as having that fourth robotic arm and gives you the freedom and mobility to kind of adjust arm placement as you need a lot of times i will use a robotic suction irrigator for cases like this however the first assist i had this day i knew was very competent and could essentially do the job of that suction irrigator and understand
the details of this case.
I am careful in the patient selections I utilize for these robotic platform sigmoidectomies and Hartman procedures as I don't do significant perforations or gross contamination.
But I have found that patients overall have a shorter length of stay, reduced incisional hernia rate, and it makes the takedown operation in the future much easier.
The surgical team prepares to enter the first abscess identified on the CT scan, with visible manipulation of surrounding tissues to access the abscess site.
Later on you'll see where we find the second abscess to enter it as well.
The surgeon performs blunt dissection, mimicking techniques used in open surgery, to separate tissue layers during the Hartmann procedure.
You know, when we get these inflamed perforated diverticulitis, a lot of times we do most of the dissection with our hands.
And having the robotic instruments essentially lets us make the same motions.
The surgeon uses a black load stapler to transect the rectum, with the option to dissect further down for additional transection.
However, I find that doing that at the takedown operation is much better whenever you're not dealing with this significant amount of inflammation.
And I knew based on the CT scan that this patient had a second abscess in this area.
The surgeon enters the abscess cavity to drain its contents, using surgical instruments to facilitate the procedure.
The surgeon tags the rectal stump with a suture, marking it for easy identification during a future takedown operation.
A lot of times I now just suture with the vessel sailor itself to save instrument cost.
The surgeon visually exposes and brings out the rectal stump during the procedure.
The surgeon transects the colon at the skin level, preparing to create an ostomy after removing the diseased portion of the colon.
I hope you enjoyed this video and see what's possible with the robotic platform.
I'm hoping to shoot a series of these kinds of videos in the future.
